coredata.dc:title: Solid-state synthesis of self-functional carbon quantum dots for detection of bacteria and tumor cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Lai, Irving Po-Jung
Harroun, Scott G.
Chen, Shiow-Yi
Unnikrishnan, Binesh
Li, Yu-Jia
Huang, Chih-Ching
coredata.dc:description: We have developed a simple solid-state synthesis procedure to functionalize fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), using ammonium citrate as a carbon source and appropriate molecules as recognition ligands. Mannose and folic acid were used to modify CQDs to selectively label Escherichia coli (E. coli) and tumor cells, respectively. First, fluorescent-core CQDs (approximate size: 3nm) were synthesized through carbonization of ammonium citrate via dry heating. In the second step, CQDs were heated with mannose and folic acid to prepare mannose-functionalized CQDs (Man–CQDs) and folic acid-functionalized CQDs (FA–CQDs), respectively, through a dehydration reaction in the solid state. Solid-state synthesis of the self-functional CQDs is achievable without a coupling agent. We optimized the labeling efficiencies of self-functional Man–CQD and FA–CQD to cells by controlling the ratio of mannose or folic acid to CQDs, as well as the reaction temperature during synthesis. The solid-state synthesized Man–CQDs and FA–CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence with excitation and emission maxima of 365 and 450nm, respectively, and a fluorescence quantum yield of approximately 9%. Man–CQDs can be used for selective labeling of E. coli and detection at concentrations as low as 100 colony forming units mL−1 in real samples (e.g., drinking water, apple juice, urine). Furthermore FA–CQDs are highly selective for labeling of folate receptor-overexpressing tumor cells. The synthesis of self-functional CQDs is simple, cost effective, and easily scaled up, and can be extended to the synthesis of various functional carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene oxide nanosheets, carbon nanotubes, fullerene nanoparticles and carbon nanodiamonds, with different ligands for other biolabeling applications and targeted therapies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Solid-state synthesis
Labeling
Bacteria
Tumor cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of biofunctional carbon quantum dots
Flow cytometric quantification of FA–CQD uptake
Labelling of tumor cells by FA–CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan under the contracts 104-2628-M-019-001-MY3, 103-2627-M-007-002-MY3, and 102-2113-M-019-001-MY3. We appreciate the assistance of Ms. Ya-Yun Yang and Ms. Ching-Yen Lin from the Instrument Center of National Taiwan University (NTU) with TEM measurements. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical representation of the synthesis of (A) mannose-functionalized carbon quantum dots (Man–CQDs) and (B) folic acid-functionalized carbon quantum dots (FA–CQDs) for the selective labeling of (A) E. coli and (B) tumor cells.

(A) TEM images, (B) UV–vis absorption spectra, and (C) fluorescence spectra of prepared (a) CQDs and (b) Man–CQDs. Inset to (A) High resolution TEM images of (a) CQDs and (b) Man–CQDs. Insets to (B and C) Photographs of the corresponding solutions (B) before and (C) after excitation with a UV lamp (365nm). Fluorescence spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 365nm. The concentrations of CQDs and Man–CQDs used for the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence analyses were 75μgmL−1. The fluorescence intensities (I F) are plotted in arbitrary units.

Fluorescence intensities (at 450nm; I F450) of E. coli (10–1.0×108 CFUmL−1) spiked (A) tap water, (B) apple juice, and (C) human urine samples, after Man–CQDs (75μgmL−1) labeling. Error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated experiments.

Bright-field (a) and (b–d) fluorescence images upon excitation with (b) long-wavelength UV light (360–380nm), (c) blue light (460–480nm), and (d) green light (510–530nm) of (A, B) HeLa cells (1.5×105 cells per well) and (C, D) A549 cells (1.5×105 cells per well) after treatment with the (A, C) CQDs (75μgmL−1) or (B, D) FA–CQDs (75μgmL−1) in cell medium for 2h. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Solid-state synthesis of self-functional carbon quantum dots for detection of bacteria and tumor cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, National Taiwan Ocean University, Keelung, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for the Oceans, National Taiwan Ocean University, Keelung, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for the Oceans

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, College of Pharmacy, Kaohsiung Medical University, Kaohsiung, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, National Taiwan Ocean University, 2, Pei-Ning Road, Keelung 20224, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have developed a simple solid-state synthesis procedure to functionalize fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), using ammonium citrate as a carbon source and appropriate molecules as recognition ligands. Mannose and folic acid were used to modify CQDs to selectively label Escherichia coli (E. coli) and tumor cells, respectively. First, fluorescent-core CQDs (approximate size: 3nm) were synthesized through carbonization of ammonium citrate via dry heating. In the second step, CQDs were heated with mannose and folic acid to prepare mannose-functionalized CQDs (Man–CQDs) and folic acid-functionalized CQDs (FA–CQDs), respectively, through a dehydration reaction in the solid state. Solid-state synthesis of the self-functional CQDs is achievable without a coupling agent. We optimized the labeling efficiencies of self-functional Man–CQD and FA–CQD to cells by controlling the ratio of mannose or folic acid to CQDs, as well as the reaction temperature during synthesis. The solid-state synthesized Man–CQDs and FA–CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence with excitation and emission maxima of 365 and 450nm, respectively, and a fluorescence quantum yield of approximately 9%. Man–CQDs can be used for selective labeling of E. coli and detection at concentrations as low as 100 colony forming units mL−1 in real samples (e.g., drinking water, apple juice, urine). Furthermore FA–CQDs are highly selective for labeling of folate receptor-overexpressing tumor cells. The synthesis of self-functional CQDs is simple, cost effective, and easily scaled up, and can be extended to the synthesis of various functional carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene oxide nanosheets, carbon nanotubes, fullerene nanoparticles and carbon nanodiamonds, with different ligands for other biolabeling applications and targeted therapies.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial, are quasi-spherical nanoparticles with sizes ranging from 3 to 10nm, and are comprised mainly of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [1–3]. CQDs have become attractive labeling agents because of their controllable emission wavelengths, high quantum yields (QYs), excellent photobleaching resistance, and good biocompatibility [4–7]. Accordingly, CQDs have been widely used to label bacteria, fungi, and tumor cells [8–15]. However, the complex processes of passivation of CQDs and anchoring of recognition molecules using toxic and/or expensive coupling agents and tedious separations are indispensable in the functionalization of CQDs for selective targeting [16–24]. Moreover, decrease in the fluorescence QYs and aqueous-phase dispersibility of CQDs usually arise following complicated modifications and separations. Thus, the development of simple synthesis methods for the biofunctionalization of CQDs with high QYs and dispersibility remains a significant challenge.

Herein, we report a simple synthesis approach comprising a two-step dry heating reaction for the preparation of self-functional CQDs (Scheme 1 ). In this process, the cores of fluorescent CQDs (size: ca. 3nm) were synthesized by heating solid-state ammonium citrate. The functional ligand shells were formed by anchoring mannose or folic acid molecules to the CQD surfaces through a dehydration reaction under dry heating. Both the prepared mannose-functionalized CQDs (Man–CQDs) and folic acid-functionalized CQDs (FA–CQDs) exhibited bright blue fluorescence and high labeling efficiency for their respective targets, Escherichia coli (E. coli) and folate receptor-overexpressing tumor cells. Compared with the one-step synthesized Man–CQDs described in our previous report [25], the two-step synthesized Man–CQDs exhibited higher efficiency in the labeling of E. coli. In the one-step method, both the formation of CQDs and the functionalization took place simultaneously, and the mechanism of formation of mannose-functionalized CQDs is ambiguous. However, in the present work we demonstrated the formation of CQDs took place in first step followed by its functionalization process in second step. In this case, it is logical to substantiate that the CQDs and functional molecules formed a core–shell structure. Furthermore, the ligand density (mannose or folic acid) on the CQDs is controllable in the two-step synthesis. These self-functional CQDs can be synthesized from the solid state without any coupling agents. In addition, the fluorescent properties and stability of CQDs did not change after functionalization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of biofunctional carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: First, CQDs were synthesized using a simple heating method. Briefly, 2g of ammonium citrate powder was placed in a 20mL beaker and heated in an oven at 180°C for 2h to yield a black residue. The formed CQDs were cooled to room temperature, ground to a fine powder, and stored in a dry cabinet for future use. To synthesize Man–CQDs, a crucible containing prepared CQDs (0.05g) and mannose (0.005g) was placed in an oven and heated at 180°C for 2h. The resulting black residue was cooled to room temperature (25°C) and dissolved in NaOH solution (100mM, 5mL). This solution was sonicated for 1h and subsequently centrifuged at a relative centrifugal force (RCF) of 35,000× g for 1h to remove large particles. The supernatant from this solution was stored at 4°C when not in use. The concentration of the purified Man–CQDs (dialysis) in the solution (in terms of mgmL−1) was determined by vacuum drying method.

FA–CQDs were synthesized using a similar method. Briefly, CQDs (0.05g) and folic acid (0.10g) were placed in a crucible and heated at 200°C for 2h in an oven. The resulting black residue (FA–CQDs) was dissolved in NaOH solution (100mM, 5mL), sonicated for 1h and dialyzed in ultrapure water (∼1L) through a dialysis membrane (MWCO=500–1000D, Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominquez, CA, USA) for 6h, and the water was replaced with ultrapure water every hour. We also synthesized CQDs subjected to a second dry-heating step without mannose or folic acid functionalization using the above described method for comparative studies.

Bacterial culturing and sample handling were performed in a sterile clean room, and all equipments were autoclaved at a high temperature and pressure to ensure sterilization. Single E. coli K12 colonies were lifted from LB agar plates and inoculated in LB media (10mL). Cultures were grown at 37°C with shaking (200rpm) until the absorbance at 600nm (OD600) reached 1.0 (optical path length: 1.0cm). A portion of each cell mixture (1.0mL) was centrifuged (RCF 3000× g, 10min, 25°C) and washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 10mM Na2HPO4, 140mM NaCl, and 2.70mM KCl; adjusted to pH 7.4 using HCl; 1mL) prior to further use. The cells were diluted to a concentration of 1.0×102–1.0×108 colony forming units (CFU) mL−1 and subsequently incubated with Man–CQDs (75μgmL−1) for 1h under gentle shaking at room temperature. The mixtures were then centrifuged (RCF 3000× g, 20min) and washed twice with PBS. The bacterial pellets were finally resuspended in PBS, and transferred separately into 96-well microtiter plates; fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Synergy 4 multi-mode microplate spectrophotometer (Biotek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA) with an excitation wavelength of 365nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometric quantification of FA–CQD uptake
ce:section.ce:para: For flow cytometric analysis of HeLa and A549 cells, 1.5×105 cells were seeded in 12-well plates and incubated overnight. Next, fresh medium containing CQDs (75μgmL−1) or FA–CQDs (75μgmL−1) was added to each well, and the cells were then incubated at 37°C for an additional 2h to allow uptake of the CQDs or FA–CQDs. After incubation, the medium was removed and the cells were washed twice with PBS (1.0mL). The cells were collected with a cell scraper and subjected to analysis on a flow cytometer (BD FACS Aria III, San Jose, CA, USA). The excitation wavelength was 488nm, and the FITC fluorescence channel was used to detect CQD or FA–CQD uptake by the cells. Untreated cells incubated with DMEM or RPMI medium alone were used as controls.
Details of the materials used in this work, characterization of Man–CQDs and FA–CQDs, analysis of E. coli in real samples, cell culture, and fluorescence imaging are given in the Supplementary data.

First, the core CQDs were prepared by heating dry-state ammonium citrate (2g) at 180°C for 2h [26]. In the second step, finely ground CQDs (0.05g) and mannose (0.005g) were well-mixed and heated at 180°C for another 2h. The synthesized brown Man–CQD product was then dispersed using NaOH (0.1M, 5mL; inset in Fig. 1 B) for a yield of approximately 70% (7.5mgmL−1). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (Fig. 1A) reveal that the particle sizes of as-prepared of CQDs (3.1±1.5nm; determined from 100 counts) and Man–CQDs (diameter: 3.2±1.2nm; determined from 100 counts) are almost the same, indicating that aggregation of particles did not take place during the functionalization process. The lattice structures of the CQDs and Man–CQDs featured d-spacing of approximately 0.34nm (insets to Fig. 1A), consistent with the 002 plane (2θ =26°) of graphitic carbon in an XRD pattern, as shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary data). Both CQDs and Man–CQDs exhibited a broad absorption band at approximately 335nm (Fig. 1B), which was attributed to overlapping n → π* transitions in the CO and π → π* in the conjugated CC bonds [27]. In addition, both CQDs and Man–CQDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission features (Fig. S2, Supplementary data) and maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of 365/450nm, with QYs of 11.5% and 8.8% (relative to quinine), respectively (Fig. 1C). These QYs are comparable to those previously reported for other N-doped CQDs [28–32]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Fig. S3), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Fig. S4), and laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (LDI-TOF MS; Fig. S5) analyses clearly demonstrated that the mannose molecules were modified on the CQDs (please see Supplementary data). In addition, we observed anhydride CO vibrations (1761cm−1) in the Man–CQDs (Fig. S4), suggesting that the mannose molecules likely anchored to CQDs via dehydration coupling between the carboxylic acid (CQDs) and hydroxyl (mannose) functional groups. The slight decrease in absorption and fluorescence intensity (Fig. 1), and reduction of fluorescence lifetime in the CQD (Fig. S6, Supplementary data) following mannose anchoring suggests that the optical properties of CQDs not only depend on the core structures, but also on their surface components.

We performed proof-of-concept experiments to evaluate the fluorescent labeling of E. coli with Man–CQDs through specific and multivalent interactions between the mannose units on CQDs and FimH lectin units in wild-type pili on the surface of E. coli [33]. Fig. S7 (Supplementary data) presents blue, green, and red bacterial images at different excitation wavelengths, indicating that Man–CQDs selectively targeted E. coli. In addition, we noted that the labeling efficiency of the two-step synthesized Man–CQDs (Fig. S8, Supplementary data) for E. coli (105 CFU ml−1) was far superior to that of our previously reported one-step synthesized Man–CQDs (synthesis via heating a solid mixture of ammonium citrate and mannose) [25], reflecting the higher mannose ligand density on the former. Man–CQDs prepared from CQDs (0.05g) and mannose (0.005g) provided the best labeling efficiency for E. coli (Fig. S9, Supplementary data) among the other mass ratios we tried, probably due to high surface mannose ligand content. The uptake concentration of optimized Man–CQDs (prepared from 0.05g of CQDs and 0.005g of mannose) in E. coli was determined by comparing the measured fluorescence of the media after incubation, with that of the control (without E. coli). The uptake concentration of optimized Man–CQDs in the E. coli (1×109 CFUmL−1) in PBS solution (pH 7.4) and at an incubation period of 1h was approximately 14.3μgmL−1. The weaker labeling efficiencies of Man–CQDs prepared from 0.05g of CQDs and a greater amount mannose (>0.005g) presumably resulted from a weaker fluorescence intensity (Fig. S10) and the interaction of E. coli with free mannose (unreacted to CQDs) rather than with the Man–CQDs. A competitive labeling assay for E. coli (108 CFUmL−1) involving Man–CQDs (0.75μgmL−1) in the presence of mannose, glucose, fructose, sucrose, or lactose (Fig. S11, Supplementary data) found that mannose (0.1gL−1) strongly inhibited Man–CQD–E. coli interactions (>60% decrease). In addition, CQDs conjugated to other saccharides (fructose, lactose, glucose, or galactose) exhibited much weaker binding affinities for E. coli compared with that of Man–CQDs (Fig. S12, Supplementary data). Moreover, among tested bacteria [E. coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus, and Salmonella enteritidis (Fig. S13, Supplementary data)], Man–CQDs only selectively labeled the E. coli, which could be ascribed to the strong multivalent binding between the mannose units and FimH proteins on the tips of E. coli type 1 fimbriae [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Labelling of tumor cells by FA–CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: We further synthesized FA–CQDs with the intent to label tumor cells, as the folate receptor is overexpressed on cells in many types of human cancer cells, including ovarian, cervical, breast, lung, kidney, brain, and colorectal malignancies, and is only minimally expressed on most normal cells [34,35]. Fluorescence images of human cervical cancer cells (HeLa cell line) labeled with optimized FA–CQDs (prepared from 0.05g of CQDs and 0.10g of folic acid at 200°C for 2h) exhibited excitation-wavelength dependence, revealing that the FA–CQDs could penetrate cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis to localize in the cytoplasm (Fig. 3 ). In contrast, the blue, green, and red fluorescence intensities of folate receptor-deficient A549 cancer cells (a human lung cancer cell line) were only slightly stronger than the auto-fluorescence of these cells under various excitation wavelengths. The QY of FA–CQDs was determined to be 9.2% (reference: quinine). The absorption spectrum of FA–CQDs displayed absorption bands (283nm) characteristic of the π→π* transition of the pterin ring of folic acid, which originated from FA (curve b in Fig. S15A, Supplementary data), indicating the successful conjugation of FA with CQDs. The folic acid in FA–CQDs was quantified by standard addition method by measuring its characteristic absorption at 283nm. We determined that the amount of folic acid conjugated on CQDs was approximately 150μgmg−1. In addition, FT-IR spectra revealed a peak at 1485–1519cm−1 (phenyl and pterin ring) in the FA–CQDs (Fig. S16, Supplementary data), further supporting the conjugation of FA molecules on CQDs. Moreover, characteristic amide peaks at 1643cm−1 indicated a newly formed amide bond between FA and CQDs, suggesting the occurrence of a dehydration reaction between the carboxylic group of FA and amine groups on CQDs. However, bands were also observed at 1381cm−1 and 1184cm−1 (COC stretching) in the FA–CQDs; therefore, we cannot exclude the potential formation of acid anhydride bonds between FA and the CQDs.

Flow cytometric results revealed that FA–CQDs were taken up by HeLa cells to a greater extent than unlabeled CQDs, and that this enhanced uptake was eliminated when the folate receptor was blocked via folic acid pre-treatment (Fig. S17, Supplementary data). The uptake concentration of FA–CQDs in HeLa cancer cells (2.0×105 cells per well; 1.0mL) in culture medium for an incubation time of 2h was found to be approximately 28.5μgmL−1. These studies provide additional evidence for the role of folic acid functionalization in increasing CQD uptake in HeLa cells. Taken together, these results demonstrate that FA–CQDs specifically target folate receptor-overexpressing tumor cells. As revealed in Fig. S18 (Supplementary data), selective labeling by FA–CQDs allowed the detection of as few as 100 HeLa cells. Our FA–CQDs therefore demonstrate strong potential for clinical tumor tissue targeting and imaging applications.

We have demonstrated a simple dry-heating method for self-functionalizing CQDs with different molecules, thus facilitating biolabeling. The fluorescent properties of CQDs, Man–CQDs, and FA–CQDs did not differ greatly, indicating that functionalization did not affect the core structure of the CQD. The dry-heating method readily facilitated the anchoring of functional ligands to the CQDs via a dehydration coupling reaction. The synthesized Man–CQDs and FA–CQDs exhibited highly efficient cell labeling with high specificity. Therefore, we believe that these self-functionalized CQDs are highly promising nanomaterials for cell labeling and tissue imaging. In addition, different functionalized carbon nanomaterials, intended to target different molecules, could be synthesized using this dry-heating method.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan under the contracts 104-2628-M-019-001-MY3, 103-2627-M-007-002-MY3, and 102-2113-M-019-001-MY3. We appreciate the assistance of Ms. Ya-Yun Yang and Ms. Ching-Yen Lin from the Instrument Center of National Taiwan University (NTU) with TEM measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.01.062.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
Bioimaging based on fluorescent carbon dots
Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Nano-carrier for gene delivery and bioimaging based on carbon dots with PEI-passivation enhanced fluorescence
Carbon-based quantum dots for fluorescence imaging of cells and tissues
Bioimaging of hyaluronic acid derivatives using nanosized carbon dots
A simple one-step method to prepare fluorescent carbon dots and their potential application in non-invasive glioma imaging
Bacterial detection with amphiphilic carbon dots
One-step hydrothermal approach to fabricate carbon dots from apple juice for imaging of mycobacterium and fungal cells
Employing carbon dots modified with vancomycin for assaying Gram-positive bacteria like Staphylococcus aureus
Polyethyleneimine modified fluorescent carbon dots and their application in cell labelling
Self-assembled hybrids of fluorescent carbon dots and PAMAM dendrimers for epirubicin delivery and intracellular imaging
Aqueous phase synthesis of highly luminescent, nitrogen-doped carbon dots and their application as bioimaging agents
Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Carbon quantum dots for zebrafish fluorescence imaging
Amorphous carbon dots with high two-photon fluorescence for cellular imaging passivated by hyperbranched poly(amino amine)
Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from cabbage with down- and up-conversion photoluminescence properties: excellent imaging agent for biomedical applications
Imidazole derivative-functionalized carbon dots: using as a fluorescence probe for detecting water and imaging of live cells
Distinguish cancer cells based on targeting turn-on fluorescence imaging by folate functionalized green emitting carbon dots
Super-resolution fluorescence imaging of biocompatible carbon dots
Quaternized carbon dot-modified graphene oxide for selective cell labeling-controlled nucleus and cytoplasm imaging
Swarming carbon dots for folic acid mediated delivery of doxorubicin and biological imaging
One-step synthesis of biofunctional carbon quantum dots for bacterial labeling
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Blue photoluminescence from chemically derived graphene oxide
Fluorescent N-doped carbon dots for both cellular imaging and highly-sensitive catechol detection
A green heterogeneous synthesis of N-doped carbon dots and their photoluminescence applications in solid and aqueous states
Highly luminescent N-doped carbon quantum dots as an effective multifunctional fluorescence sensing platform
Fast one-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots by pyrolyzing ethanolamine
One-pot synthesis of N-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Interdomain interaction in the FimH adhesin of Escherichia coli regulates the affinity to mannose
Structural basis for molecular recognition of folic acid by folate receptors
Engineering folate-drug conjugates to target cancer: from chemistry to clinic

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Irving Po-Jung Lai is currently doing his Doctoral studies at the Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, National Taiwan Ocean University, Taiwan. His research interest is synthesis of carbon-based nanomaterials for the biolabelling.

Scott G. Harroun is a Research Assistant in the laboratory of Dr. Huan-Tsung Chang in the Department of Chemistry at National Taiwan University. He previously earned his B.Sc. and M.Sc. degrees under the supervision of Dr. Christa Brosseau in the Department of Chemistry, Saint Mary's University, Canada. He will return to Canada in September for his doctoral studies in chemistry at the Université de Montréal. His research interests are SERS and electrochemistry for biosensing applications.

Shiow-Yi Chen is an Assistant Professor of the Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, National Taiwan Ocean University, Taiwan. Her research work is mainly focused on development of functional nanomaterials in tumor therapy.

Binesh Unnikrishnan is currently working as Postdoctoral Fellow at the Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, National Taiwan Ocean University, Taiwan. His research interests include synthesis of nanomaterials for biosensor and energy devise applications.

Yu-Jia Li is currently doing his Doctoral studies at the Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, National Taiwan Ocean University, Taiwan. Her research interest is synthesis of antibacterial nanomaterials.

Chih-Ching Huang is a Professor of the Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, National Taiwan Ocean University, Taiwan. He received his Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from the Department of Chemistry with Dr. Huan-Tsung Chang, National Taiwan University, Taiwan (2004). He worked as Postdoctoral fellow at the Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University (2006–2008). In 2008, he joined the Department of Bioscience and Biotechnology, National Taiwan Ocean University, Taiwan. His research work is mainly focused on development of nanosensors and nanodrugs.
